author: John Chrysostom
book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.

title: Homily XIX

source.title: NPNF1-09. St. Chrysostom: On the Priesthood; Ascetic Treatises; Select Homilies and Letters; Homilies on the Statutes

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: On the Sunday called “Episozomenes,” to those who had come to Antioch from the country—also on the subject of avoiding oaths.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Ye have revelled during the last few days in the Holy Martyrs!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Ye have taken your fill of the spiritual feast!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Ye have all exulted with honest exultation!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Ye have beheld their ribs laid bare, and their loins lacerated; the blood flowing forth all around; ten thousand forms of torture!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Ye have seen human nature exhibiting that which is above nature, and crowns woven with blood!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Ye have danced a goodly dance throughout the whole city; this, your noble captain leading you on; but sickness compelled me to remain at home, although against my will.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But if I did not take a part in the festival, I partook of the pleasure of it.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: If I could not have the enjoyment of your public assembly, yet did I share in your gladness.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For such is the power of love, that it makes those who are not actually in the enjoyment to rejoice equally with those who are; persuading them to think the good things of their neighbour common to themselves.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Therefore even whilst I sat at home, I was rejoicing with you; and now whilst I am not yet entirely freed from my sickness, I have risen up, and run to meet you, that I may see your much desired faces, and take a part in the present festival.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For I think the present day to be a very great festival indeed on account of our brethren, who by their presence beautify our city, and adorn the Church; a people foreign to us in language, but in harmony with us concerning the faith, a people passing their time in tranquillity, and leading an honest and sober life.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For among these men there are no spectacles of iniquity—no horse racings, nor harlots, nor any of that riot which pertains to a city, but every kind of licentiousness is banished, and great sobriety flourishes every where.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And the reason is, that their life is a laborious one; and they have, in the culture of the soil, a school of virtue and sobriety, and follow that art which God introduced before all others into our life.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For before the sin of Adam, when he enjoyed much freedom, a certain tillage of the ground was enjoined upon him; not indeed a laborious or a troublesome one, but one which afforded him much good discipline, for he was appointed, it is said, “to till the garden, and to keep it.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Each of these men you may see at one time employed in yoking the labouring oxen, and guiding the plough, and cutting the deep furrow; and at another ascending the sacred pulpit, and cultivating the souls of those under their authority; at one time cutting away the thorns from the soil with a bill-hook, at another purging out the sins of the soul by the Word.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For they are not ashamed of work like the inhabitants of our city, but they are ashamed of idleness, knowing that this has taught every kind of wickedness; and that to those who love it, it has proved a teacher of iniquity from the beginning.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: These are our philosophers, and theirs the best philosophy, exhibiting their virtue not by their outward appearance, but by their mind.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: The pagan philosophers are in character no wise better than those who are engaged on the stage, and in the sports of actors; and they have nothing to shew beyond the threadbare cloak, the beard, and the long robe!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But these, quite on the contrary, bidding farewell to staff and beard, and the other accoutrements, have their souls adorned with the doctrines of the true philosophy, and not only with the doctrines, but also with the real practice.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And were you to question any one of these, who live a rustic life at the spade and plough, as to the dogmas respecting which the pagan philosophers have discoursed an infinite deal, and have expended a multitude of words, without being able to say any thing sound; one of these would give you an accurate reply from his store of wisdom.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And not only is this to be wondered at, but that they confirm the credibility of these doctrines by their actions.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For of the fact that we have an immortal soul, and that we shall hereafter render an account of what we have done here, and stand before a fearful Tribunal, their minds are at once thoroughly persuaded, and they have also regulated their whole course of life by such hopes as these; and have become superior to all worldly show, instructed as they have been by the sacred Scriptures, that “all is vanity, yea, vanity of vanities,” and they do not greedily long for any of those things which seem to be so splendid.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: These too know how to philosophize concerning God, even as God hath determined; and if, taking one of them, you were now to bring forward some pagan philosopher;—or rather, now you could not find one!—But if you were to take one of these, and then open the books of their ancient philosophers, and go through them, and institute an enquiry by way of parallel as to what these now answer, and the others in their day philosophically advanced; you would see how much wisdom belonged to the former, and how much folly to the latter.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For whilst some of those would aver, that the things existing were destitute of a providence, and that the creation had not its origin from God; that virtue was not sufficient for itself, but stood in need of wealth, and nobility, and external splendour, and other things still more ridiculous; and whilst these, on the other hand, would discourse wisely respecting Providence, respecting the future Tribunals of judgment, respecting the creative power of God, bringing forth all things out of nothing, as well as respecting all other points, although at the same time they were entirely destitute of worldly schooling; who could but learn from hence the power of Christ, which hath proved these unlearned and simple persons to be as much wiser than those, who make so much boast of their wisdom, as men of discretion are seen to be in comparison of little children?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For what harm can result to them from their simplicity in regard to learning, when their thoughts are full of much wisdom?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And what advantage have those philosophers from this learning, when the understanding is devoid of right thoughts?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: It were just as if one should have a sword that had its hilt of silver, whilst the blade was weaker than the vilest lead.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For truly these philosophers have their tongue decked out with words and names, but their understanding is full of mere weakness and good for nothing.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Not so with these philosophers, but quite the reverse.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Their understanding is full of spiritual wisdom and their mode of life is a transcript of their doctrines.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Amongst these there are no luxurious women; there are no ornaments of dress, nor colours, nor paints; but all such corruption of manners is discountenanced.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Hence the population under their charge are the more readily trained to sobriety, and the law which Paul gave, when he directed that food and covering should be had, and nothing more be sought after, they most rigidly observe.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Amongst them, there are no perfumed unguents to fascinate the senses; but the earth bringing forth herbs, prepares for them a varied fragrance of flowers, above all the skill of perfumers.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For this reason, their bodies as well as souls enjoy a sound state of health, inasmuch as they have banished all luxury of diet, and driven off all the evil floods of drunkenness; and they eat just as much as suffices for subsistence.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Let us then not despise them because of their outward appearance, but let us admire their mind.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For of what advantage is the external habit, when the soul is more wretchedly clad than any beggar!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: The man ought to be praised and admired, not for dress, nay more, not for his bodily form, but for his soul.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Lay bare the soul of these men, and you will see its beauty and the wealth it possesses, in their words, in their doctrines, and in the whole system of their manners!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Let the Gentiles then be ashamed, let them hide their heads, and slink away on account of their philosophers, and their wisdom, wretched as it is beyond all folly!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For the philosophers that have been amongst them in their lifetime have hardly been able to teach their doctrines to a very few, who can easily be numbered; and when any trifling peril overtook them, they lost even these.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But the disciples of Christ, the fishermen, the publicans, and the tent-makers, in a few years brought over the whole world to the truth; and when from that time, ten thousand perils have been constantly arising, the preaching of the Gospel was so far from being put down, that it still flourishes and increases; and they taught simple people, tillers of the ground, and occupied with cattle, to be lovers of wisdom.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Such are the persons, who beside all the rest having deeply rooted in them that love which is the source of all good things, have hastened to us, undertaking so long a journey, that they might come and embrace their fellow-members.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Come then, and in return for these favours, (I speak of their love and kind feeling), let us give them a provision, and so send them home; and let us again raise the question concerning oaths; that from the minds of all we may pluck up by the roots this evil custom.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But first, I desire to put you a little in mind to-day of the things we spoke of lately.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: When the Jews, having been released from Persia, and set free from that tyranny, were returned back to their own county, “I saw,” saith one, “a flying sickle, twenty cubits in length, and ten cubits broad.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: They heard also the Prophet giving them this instruction, “This is the curse, that goeth forth over the face of the whole land, and entereth into the house of him that sweareth falsely; and it shall rest in the midst thereof, and throw down the timber and all the stones.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: When we had read this passage, we also enquired then why it was, that it should destroy not the swearer only, but also his house, and we stated this to be the reason; that God will have the punishments of the most grievous sins to remain continually visible; that all may afterwards learn prudence.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Inasmuch then as it was necessary that the perjurer when dead should be buried, and committed to the bosom of the earth; in order that his wickedness might not be buried along with him, his house was made a heap, so that all who passed by, beholding it, and learning the reason of the overthrow, might avoid imitating the sin.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: This also happened at Sodom.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For when they burned in their lust one towards another, then too the very earth itself was burned up, being kindled by the fire from above.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For He designed, that the vengeance of this sin should permanently remain.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And observe the mercy of God!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Those who had sinned, He caused not to continue burning to the present day, but when they had been for once in flames, He buried them; and burning up the face of the ground, He placed it visibly before all who after should desire to look at these things; and now the sight of the land, through all the generations since, hath given an admonition beyond all powers of speech, crying out as it were, and saying, “Dare not to do the deeds of Sodom, lest ye suffer the lot of Sodom!”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For precept commonly makes not so deep an impression upon the mind as a fearful spectacle does, which bears upon it the vestiges of calamity though all time.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And persons that have visited these places bear witness, who often, when they hear the Scripture discoursing of these things, are not much terrified; but when they have gone and stood upon the site, and see the whole surface of it disfigured, and have witnessed the effects of the fire, with soil no where visible, but every thing dust and ashes, they come away astonished with the sight, and taking with them a strong lesson of chastity.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For truly, the very nature of the punishment was a pattern of the nature of the sin!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Even as they devised a barren intercourse, not having for its end the procreation of children, so did God bring on them such a punishment, as made the womb of the land ever barren, and destitute of all fruits!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For this reason also He threatened to destroy the dwellings of the swearers, in order that by their punishments, they may make others to be more self-controlled.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But I am ready to shew to-day, not the destruction of one, two, or three houses in consequence of oaths, but that of a whole city and of a people beloved of God; of a nation that had always enjoyed much of the divine care; and of a race that had escaped many dangers.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For Jerusalem herself, the city of God, which had the holy ark, and all that divine service;—where there were once prophets, and the grace of the Spirit, and the ark; and the tables of the covenant, and the golden pot;—where angels were frequent visitors;—this city, I say, when a multitude of wars took place, and many foreign nations made attacks upon it, as if girt by a wall of adamant, ever laughed them all to scorn, and whilst the land was utterly destroyed, sustained no injury!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And not only is this to be wondered at, but that frequently in driving out its enemies, it inflicted upon them a heavy blow, and enjoyed so much of the providential care of God, that God Himself said, “I found Israel as a bunch of grapes in the desert; and I beheld your fathers as the earliest fruit on the fig tree.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And again, of the city itself: “As olive berries on the extremity of the highest bough, and they shall say, Do them no harm.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Nevertheless, the city beloved of God; that had escaped so many perils; that had been favoured with pardon, amidst the multitude of its sins; that alone had been able to avoid captivity, whilst all the rest were carried away, not once or twice, but very often; was ruined solely by an oath.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But how, I proceed to state.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: One of their kings was Zedekiah.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: This Zedekiah took an oath to Nebuchadnezzar, king of the barbarians, that he would remain in alliance with him.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Afterwards he revolted, and went over to the king of Egypt, disdaining the obligation of his oath, and suffered the things of which ye shall hear presently.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But first, it is necessary to mention the parable of the prophet, in which he enigmatically represented all these matters: “The word of the Lord,” saith he, “came to me, saying, Son of man, put forth a riddle, and speak a parable, and say, Thus saith the Lord God: A great eagle, with great wings, and long extended, full of claws.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Here he calls the king of the Babylonians an eagle, and speaks of him as being “great, and long-winged;” and he calls him long-extended and “full of claws,” on account of the multitude of his army, and the greatness of his power, and the swiftness of his invasion.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For just as the wings and claws of the eagle are his armour, so are horses and soldiers to kings.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: This eagle, he goes on to say, “hath the leading to enter into Lebanon.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: What is meant by the “leading?”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Counsel—design.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And Judæa is called Lebanon, because of its situation near that mountain.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Afterwards, intending to speak of the oaths and treaties, “He took,” saith he, “of the seed of the land, and planted it in a fruitful field, that it might take root by great waters.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: He placed it to be looked upon; and it grew, and became a weak vine, and of small stature, and it stretched out its branches towards him, and its roots were under him.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Here he calls the city of Jerusalem a vine; but in saying that it stretched out its branches towards the eagle, and that its roots were under him, he refers to the treaties and alliances made with him; and that it cast itself upon him.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Next, purposing to declare the iniquity of this, he saith, “And there was another great eagle,” (speaking of the Egyptian king), “with great wings, and having many claws; and the vine did bend itself toward him, and its tendril toward him, and shot out its branches, that it might be watered.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Therefore, I said, Thus saith the Lord God: Shall it prosper?”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: That is to say, “after having broken the oath, and the treaties, shall it be able to remain, or to be safe, or to avoid falling?”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Presently, for the purpose of shewing that this is not to happen, but that it is certainly to be destroyed on account of the oath, he discourses concerning its punishment, and alleges the cause.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: “For its tender roots and its fruits shall become corrupt, and all which springs therefrom shall be withered.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And for the purpose of shewing that it will not be destroyed by human strength, but because it hath made God its enemy by means of these oaths, he subjoins, “Not by a mighty arm, nor by much people, to pluck it up by its roots.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Such indeed is the parable, but the prophet again explains it, when he says, “Behold, the king of Babylon cometh against Jerusalem.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And then, after saying some other things between, he mentions the oaths and the treaties.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: “For” saith he, “he shall make a covenant with him;” and presently, speaking of the departure from it, he goes on to say, “And he will depart from him, by sending messengers into Egypt, that they might give him horses and much people.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And then he proceeds to shew that it is on account of the oath that all this destruction is to take place.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: “Surely in the place where the king dwelleth that made him king, he who hath despised My curse, and hath transgressed My covenant, in the midst of Babylon he shall die; and not by great power nor by multitude, because he despised the oath in transgressing this My covenant; I will surely recompense upon his own head this My oath which he hath dishonoured, and My covenant which he hath broken; and I will spread My net upon him.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Seest thou, that not once, or twice, but repeatedly, it is said that because of the oath he was to suffer all these things.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For God is inexorable when oaths are treated contemptuously.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Nor merely from the punishment which was brought upon the city by the oath, but also from the delay, and the postponement, may it be seen how much God is concerned for the inviolability of oaths.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: “For it came to pass,” we are told, “in the ninth year of the reign of Zedekiah, on the tenth day of the month, that Nebuchadnezzar the king of Babylon came, and all his host, against Jerusalem, and pitched against it, and built a wall against it round about, and the city was besieged until the eleventh year of king Zedekiah, and the ninth day of the month, and there was no bread for the people to eat, and the city was broken up.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: He might indeed, at once from the first day, have delivered them up, and have given them into the hands of their enemies; but He permitted that they should first be wasted for the space of three years, and experience a most distressing siege; to the end that during this interval, being humbled by the terror of the forces without, or the famine that oppressed the city within, they might compel the king, however unwillingly, to submit to the barbarian; and some alleviation might be obtained for the sin committed.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And to prove that this is true, and no conjecture of my own, hear what He saith to him by the prophet: “If thou shalt go forth to the king of Babylon’s princes, then thy soul shall live, and this city shall not be burned with fire; and thou shalt live, and thine house.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But if thou wilt not go forth to the king of Babylon’s princes, then shall this city be given into the hand of the Chaldeans; and they shall burn it with fire, and thou shalt not escape out of their hand.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And the king said, I am afraid of the Jews that are fallen to the Chaldeans, lest they deliver me into their hands and they mock me.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But Jeremiah said, They shall not deliver thee.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Obey, I beseech thee, the word of the Lord, which I speak unto thee; so shall it be better for thee, and thy soul shall live.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But if thou refuse to go forth, this is the word that the Lord hath shewed me.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: All the women that are left in the king of Judah’s house, shall be brought forth to the king of Babylon’s princes; and those shall say, The men who are at peace with thee have deceived thee, and have prevailed over thee; they shall prevail when thy feet slip; they are turned away from thee, and they shall bring out all thy wives, and thy children to the Chaldeans, and thou shalt not escape out of their hand, for thou shalt be taken by the hand of the king of Babylon, and this city shall be burned with fire.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But when He did not prevail with him by this address, but he remained in his sin and transgression, after three years, God delivered up the city, displaying at once His own clemency and the ingratitude of that king.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And entering in with the utmost ease, they “burnt the house of the Lord, and the king’s house, and the houses of Jerusalem, and every great house, the captain of the guard burnt, and overthrew the wall of Jerusalem;” and everywhere there was the fire of the barbarian, the oath being the conductor of the conflagration, and carrying about the flame in all directions.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: “And the captain of the guard carried away the rest of the people that were left in the city, and the fugitives that fell away to the king of Babylon.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And the pillars of brass that were in the house of the Lord the Chaldeans brake up, and the bases, and the brazen sea that was in the house of the Lord, did the Chaldees break in pieces.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And the pots, and the flesh-hooks, and the bowls, and the censers, and all the vessels of brass wherewith they ministered, took they away.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And the firepans, and all the golden and silver bowls they took away.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Moreover, Nebuzaradan, the captain of the guard, took away the two pillars, and the bases, and the sea which Solomon had made in the house of the Lord.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And they took away Seraiah the chief priest, and Zephaniah the second priest, and the three keepers of the door; and out of the city one eunuch that was set over the men of war; and five men that were in the king’s presence; and Shaphan the chief captain, and the principal scribe, and threescore men.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And he took these, and brought them to the king of Babylon, and the king smote them, and slew them.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Be mindful therefore, I pray, now of the “flying sickle” that “resteth in the swearer’s house;” and “destroyeth the walls and the timber and the stones.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Be mindful, I pray, how this oath entered into the city, and overturned houses, and temple, and walls, and splendid buildings, and made the city an heap; and that neither the Holy of Holies, nor the sacred vessels, nor any thing else could ward off that punishment and vengeance, for that the oath had been transgressed!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: The city, indeed, was thus miserably destroyed.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But the king endured what was still more wretched and deplorable.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And as the flying sickle overthrew the buildings, so did it also cut him down in his flight.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For “the king,” it says, “went forth by night, by way of the gate, and the Chaldeans encompassed the city, and the army of the Chaldeans pursued after the king and overtook him, and they took the king, and brought him to the king of Babylon, and the king of Babylon gave judgment upon Zedekiah, and slew his sons before his face, and put out the eyes of Zedekiah, and bound him with fetters, and carried him to Babylon.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: What is meant by the expression, “he spake judgment with him?”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: He demanded of him an account of his conduct, he pleaded against him; and first he slew his two sons, that he might be a spectator of the calamity of his house, and might behold that deplorable tragedy; and then he put out his own eyes.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For what reason, I ask again, did this occur?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: In order that he might go as a teacher to the barbarians, and too the Jews who dwelt among them; and that they who had eyes might discern by him who was bereft of sight, how great an evil is an oath!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Nor only these; but all who dwelt by the way, beholding the man fettered and blinded, might learn by his calamity the greatness of his sin.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Therefore one of the prophets declares, “He shall not see Babylon.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And another, “He shall be carried away to Babylon.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And the prophecy seems, indeed, to be contradictory.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But it is not so; for both of these are true.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For he saw not Babylon, though he was carried away to Babylon.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: How then did he not see Babylon?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Because it was in Judæa he had his eyes put out; for where the oath had been set at nought, there also was it vindicated, and he himself subjected to punishment.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And how was he carried away to Babylon?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: In a state of captivity.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For since the punishment was twofold, deprivation of sight and captivity, the prophets took them severally.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: The one saith, “He shall not see Babylon,” speaking of the loss of his eyes; the other saith, “He shall be carried away to Babylon,” signifying his captivity.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Knowing these things, then, brethren, and gathering up what has been now advanced, as well as what has been said before; let us at last desist from this evil custom, yea, I pray and beseech you all!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For if in the old dispensation, when the Jews had not the strictest moral wisdom required of them, but much condescension was extended to them, such wrath was the effect of one oath; such capture and captivity; what punishment is it likely that those who swear should now be subjected to, after an express law forbidding the practice, and so large an addition of precepts.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Is it, indeed, all that is required, that we come to the assembly, and hear what is spoken?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Why truly it is a reason for greater condemnation, and for more inevitable punishment, that we are continually hearing, and yet do not what is bidden!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: What excuse shall we have, or what pardon, if assembling here from earliest youth to latest old age, and enjoying the advantage of so much instruction, we remain just like them, and do not take pains to correct a single defect.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Let no one henceforth allege custom.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For this is the very thing at which I am indignant and provoked, that we are not able to get the better of custom.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And, pray, if we do not get the better of custom, how can we get the better of concupiscence, which hath its root even in the principles of our nature; for it is natural to feel desire; but to desire wickedly, comes after of choice.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But this practice of swearing takes not even its first principle from nature, but from mere negligence.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And that thou mayest learn that not from the difficulty of the thing, but through our inattention, this sin has advanced to such a pitch, let us call to mind how many things far more difficult than these, men accomplish; and that too without expecting any recompense therefrom.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Let us think what services the Devil imposes; how laborious, how troublesome they are; and yet, the difficulty has not become an obstacle to these services.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For what can be more difficult, I ask, than when any young person delivering himself up to those, who undertake to make his limbs supple and pliant, uses his most strenuous exertion to bend his whole body into the exact shape of a wheel, and to turn over upon the pavement; his powers being tasked at the same time through the eyes, and through the movement of the hands, as well as other convolutions for the purpose of being transformed into the likeness of woman-kind.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Yet neither the difficulty of these feats, nor the degradation arising from them, are thought of.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And again, those who are dragged upon the dancing-stage, and use the members of the body as though they were wings, who that beholds them can help being struck with wonder?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: So too they who toss knives aloft in the air one after another, and catch them all by the handle, whom might they not put to shame of those who refuse to undergo any labour for the sake of virtue?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And what can any one say of those men, who balancing a pole on the forehead, keep it just as steady as a tree rooted in the ground?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And this is not the only marvellous part of the affair but that they set little children to wrestle with one another on the top of the tree; and neither the hands, nor any other part of the body assisting, the forehead alone sustains the pole unshaken, and with more steadiness than any kind of fastening.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Again: another walks on the slenderest rope, with the same fearlessness as men do when they run over level plains.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Nevertheless these things, which even in thought seem impracticable, have become possible by art.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: What like this have we, I ask, to allege concerning oaths?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: What kind of difficulty? what toil? what art? what danger?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: There is only needed on our part a little earnestness, and the whole of our task will be quickly performed.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And do not tell me, “I have accomplished the greater part of it;” but if thou hast not accomplished the whole, consider that thou hast not as yet done any thing; for this little, if neglected, is destruction to all the rest.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Often indeed when men have built a house, and put on the roof, they have destroyed the whole fabric, by not making any concern of a single tile that has been shaken off from it.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And one may see the same thing occur with respect to garments; for there too if a small hole is made, and not repaired, a large rent is the consequence.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And this also is frequently the case in regard to floods; for these, if they find but a small entrance, let in the whole torrent.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Thou also, then, even if thou hast fortified thyself all around, and but a small part be left still unfortified, yet block up this also against the devil, that thou mayest be made strong on all sides!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Thou hast seen the sickle!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Thou hast seen the head of John!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Thou hast heard the history pertaining to Saul!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Thou hast heard the manner of the Jewish captivity!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And beside all these, thou hast heard the sentence of Christ declaring, that not only to commit perjury, but to swear in any way, is a diabolical thing, and the whole a device of the evil one.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Thou hast heard that every where perjuries follow oaths.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Putting all these things then together, write them upon thy understanding.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Dost thou not see how women and little children suspend Gospels from their necks as a powerful amulet, and carry them about in all places wherever they go.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Thus do thou write the commands of the Gospel and its laws upon thy mind.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Here there is no need of gold or property, or of buying a book; but of the will only, and the affections of the soul awakened, and the Gospel will be thy surer guardian, carrying it as thou wilt then do, not outside, but treasured up within; yea, in the soul’s secret chambers.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: When thou risest up then from thy bed, and when thou goest out of thine house, repeat this law: “I say unto you, Swear not at all.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And the saying will be to thee a discipline; for there is no need of much labour, but only of a moderate degree of attention.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And that this is true, may thus be proved.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Call thy son, and frighten him, and threaten to lay a few stripes upon him, if he does not duly observe this law; and thou wilt see, how he will forthwith abstain from this custom.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Is it not therefore truly absurd, that little children, out of the fear we inspire, should perform this commandment, and that we should not fear God as our sons fear us?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: What then I said before this, I now again repeat.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Let us lay down a law for ourselves in this matter; not to meddle either with public or private affairs until we have fulfilled this law; and then surely under the pressure of this obligation we shall easily conquer, and we shall at once adorn ourselves, and decorate our city.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For consider what a thing it would be to have it said every where throughout the world, “A practice becoming Christians is established at Antioch, and you will hear no one giving utterance to an oath, even though the greatest necessity is laid upon him!”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: This is what the neighbouring cities will certainly hear; nay, not the neighbouring cities only, but even to the ends of the earth will the report be conveyed.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For it is indeed probable that both the merchants who mix with you, and others who arrive from this place, will report all these matters.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: When, therefore, many persons in the way of encomium mention the harbours of other cities, or the markets, or the abundance of wares, enable those who come from hence to say, that there is that at Antioch, which is to be seen in no other city; for that the men who dwell there would sooner have their tongues cut out, than suffer an oath to proceed from their mouths!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: This will be your ornament and defence, and not only so, but it will bring an abundant reward.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For others also will certainly emulate, and imitate you.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But if, when a person has gained but one or two, he shall receive so great a reward from God; what recompense shall ye not receive when ye are the instructors of the whole world.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: It is your duty then to bestir yourselves, to be watchful, and to be sober; knowing that not only from our own personal good works, but from those we have also wrought in others, shall we receive the best recompense, and enjoy much favour with God, which may He grant us all continually to enjoy, and hereafter to obtain the kingdom of heaven, in Christ Jesus our Lord; to Whom with the Father, and the Holy Ghost, be glory and power both now and ever, and world without end.
